name: SLAM Pipeline
summary: The SLAM pipeline is the sequence of processing stages that transforms raw sensor data into a consistent map and accurate robot localisation. It encompasses front-end processing (feature extraction, scan matching, visual odometry), back-end optimisation (graph optimisation, loop closure correction), and map management (representation, storage, update).
whenToUseIt: Use the SLAM pipeline framework when designing or configuring a SLAM system for a mobile robot. It helps you understand which components to select, how they connect, and where the system is most likely to fail. Apply this framework when building autonomous navigation for indoor robots, underground mining vehicles, drones in GPS-denied environments, or any mobile platform that must operate without prior maps.
steps: Sensor input: Select and configure the primary SLAM sensor — 2D LiDAR (simple, fast, good for structured environments), 3D LiDAR (rich data, works in complex 3D spaces), monocular camera (cheap but scale-ambiguous), stereo camera (provides depth), or RGB-D camera (dense depth indoors). Many systems fuse multiple sensor types.

Front-end — feature extraction and matching: Extract salient features from sensor data. For LiDAR: extract corners, planes, or use scan-to-scan matching (ICP, NDT). For cameras: extract and match visual features (ORB, SIFT) or use direct methods (pixel intensity matching). The front-end produces relative motion estimates (odometry) between consecutive frames.

Front-end — odometry estimation: Combine sensor-derived motion estimates with wheel odometry and IMU data to produce a real-time estimate of the robot's trajectory. This runs at high frequency (10-100 Hz) and provides the 'current best guess' of the robot's position.

Back-end — graph construction: Build a pose graph where nodes represent robot poses at key moments (keyframes) and edges represent constraints from odometry and sensor matching. Each edge has an associated uncertainty.

Back-end — loop closure detection: When the robot revisits a location, detect the match using appearance-based methods (bag-of-words for visual SLAM, scan context for LiDAR SLAM). Add a loop closure constraint connecting the current pose to the matching historical pose.

Back-end — graph optimisation: Optimise the pose graph to find the set of poses that best satisfies all constraints (odometry and loop closures) simultaneously. Libraries like g2o, GTSAM, and Ceres Solver minimise the total error across all constraints, correcting accumulated drift.

Map output: Generate the output map — occupancy grid (2D navigation), point cloud (3D visualisation), mesh (surface reconstruction), or feature map (sparse landmarks). The map is updated as new areas are explored and existing areas are revisited.
example: Emesent's Hovermap system for autonomous underground mine mapping implements the full SLAM pipeline. A Velodyne VLP-16 LiDAR provides 3D point clouds at 10 Hz. The front-end uses LOAM (LiDAR Odometry and Mapping) to extract edge and planar features from each scan and match them to the accumulated map, producing 6-DOF odometry estimates fused with an IMU. As the drone explores, the back-end builds a pose graph. When the drone returns to previously mapped areas, a scan-context-based loop closure detector identifies the revisit and adds constraints to the graph. The graph optimiser (iSAM2) runs incrementally, maintaining a globally consistent map in real time. The output is a dense, colour-mapped 3D point cloud of the mine void at centimetre resolution.
